scenario: Stellar Fintech runs a payment authorization process on Camunda 8.8 that performs 20 REST calls per job, each averaging 100 ms. The team observes a ceiling of 10 jobs per second on a single worker thread and needs to lift throughput to roughly 100 jobs per second across the worker pool while keeping a small CPU footprint.
question: Which scaling technique does the writing-good-workers guide cite as raising blocking REST throughput from about 10 to 100 jobs per second?

options.text: Disable long polling on the worker and request a single job at a time so the broker pushes work faster than the worker can synchronously process REST calls in sequence
Pin one worker thread per CPU core and process all jobs strictly sequentially on that core to avoid context switching and maximize raw single-thread REST call throughput
Use a thread pool of 10 threads so blocked I/O threads overlap waiting time and roughly 10 times as many REST calls run in parallel against the same worker process
Move from a thread pool of 10 threads to a thread pool of 1000 threads to overlap thousands of REST calls regardless of the I/O latency or available CPU cores on the host

a.text: Incorrect. The guide promotes batching and long polling for fetching jobs; reducing batch size or disabling long polling does not lift the blocking REST throughput ceiling.

b.text: Incorrect. Strictly sequential per-core processing is the worst case for scalability; the guide raises throughput by using a thread pool of blocked I/O threads.

c.text: Correct. The guide uses a thread pool of 10 threads with 100 ms REST latency to raise throughput from 10 to 100 jobs per second because blocked I/O threads do not consume CPU cores.

d.text: Incorrect. The guide notes that sizing thread pools requires careful understanding; it recommends reactive programming over arbitrarily huge pools for further scaling.

explanation: Camunda 8.8 worker guidance shows that, given 100 ms blocking REST calls, sizing a thread pool to 10 threads overlaps I/O waits and lifts throughput from about 10 to about 100 jobs per second; reactive programming is recommended for further scaling.
